title: Chapter 10: The Garden Conversation
content: Liang Xue found him in the garden at dusk.

Ren was standing by the koi pond, watching the fat orange fish glide through water that reflected a sky streaked with purple and gold. The jade token from Hong was in his hand‚Äînot activating, just warm, a reminder that the world was stranger and more dangerous than the elegant garden suggested.
"I've been watching you for three weeks," Xue said.

He turned. She stood at the edge of the stone path, arms at her sides, the jade pendant catching the last light. No armor tonight‚Äîno blazer, no laptop, no wall of corporate formality. Just a woman in a dark dress, with her hair down and something fragile in her posture.
"You and the household security," Ren said mildly.
"Not them. *Me.*" She stepped closer. "Every morning, I review the surveillance footage from the previous day. Your movements, your contacts, your patterns. I've been doing it since the banquet."

"I know." He'd known from day two. The enhanced senses had picked up the subtle shift in camera angles every time he moved through a room‚Äîa live tracking protocol that wasn't part of the standard security rotation. Someone was tasking the cameras manually.
That someone was Xue.

"Then you know I've seen you leave the estate at midnight. Visit a tea house in the old quarter. Meet with a woman who the police have interviewed three times about her connections to missing persons cases." Xue's voice was steady, but there was an edge beneath it‚Äîthe edge of someone who'd been patient and was now demanding answers. "You're building something outside this family. An intelligence network. And you haven't told me about it."
Ren considered his options. The truth was too dangerous. A lie would collapse the trust he'd spent three weeks building. The middle path was riskiest of all‚Äîpartial truth, carefully curated.
But Xue wasn't asking for partial truth. She was asking for the truth she *needed*, whether she could handle it or not.

"The woman's name is Hong," he said. "She's an information broker who's been tracking unusual disappearances in Crystal City. Practitioners of traditional arts‚Äîpeople with a sensitivity to things most humans can't perceive. They're vanishing. Three in the past six months. All within the Liang estate's leyline range."
Xue's expression didn't change. But her hand drifted to the jade pendant again‚Äîthe tell he'd learned to read.
"I know about the disappearances," she said quietly.
Ren went still. "You know?"

"The family keeps records. Old practitioners in the region, their sensitivity levels, their locations. When they disappear, the records are updated." Her voice was flat. "I assumed it was... natural attrition. Old people dying, families not reporting. I chose not to look closer."
*You chose not to know.* The same survival strategy he'd identified, now confirmed.
"Xue. These aren't natural deaths. Something is *taking* them. And it's connected to what's beneath this estate."
The words landed like stones in still water. Xue's composure‚Äîalways her shield, her armor, her most reliable weapon‚Äîcracked. Just a fraction. Just enough for him to see the fear beneath.

"I know there's something under this estate," she said, and her voice was barely a whisper. "I've known since I was twelve years old. I woke up one night and felt the ground *humming*. I went to my father and he told me it was the family's inheritance‚Äîancient arrays that protected our lineage, that gave us our prosperity. He said it was a gift."
"It's not a gift."

"No." She met his eyes, and the rawness there made his chest tighten. "I've spent twelve years not asking questions. Because the answers might be worse than the uncertainty. Because asking might mean I'd have to *do* something, and I didn't know what." A breath. "But you keep showing me that not knowing isn't safety. It's just a different kind of danger."
Ren stepped closer. Close enough to see the tremor in her jaw, the pulse jumping at her throat, the way her fingers clenched the jade pendant hard enough to press white lines into her palm.
"I'm going to tell you something," he said. "And you're not going to believe me. But I need you to listen anyway."
"Alright."

"The arrays beneath this estate aren't protecting anything. They're *building* something. A dimensional gateway‚Äîancient, powerful, designed to bridge the gap between our reality and another dimension. A dimension inhabited by entities that view human existence the way we view anthills: something to pave over. The practitioners are disappearing because those entities‚Äîoperating through human agents‚Äîare eliminating potential opposition and harvesting bloodline energy to accelerate the gateway's construction."
He paused. Let the words settle.

"Your father isn't just the Patriarch of a wealthy family. He's the architect of a portal that, when complete, will end the world. And he's going to finish it in less than three years unless someone stops him."
Silence. The koi pond gurgled. Somewhere in the estate, a clock chimed seven.
Xue's face cycled through emotions‚Äîdisbelief, fear, anger, denial. The War God in Ren braced for rejection. For the screaming. For the cold dismissal.
Instead, Xue closed her eyes.
When she opened them, the fear was still there. But so was something harder. Steel forged in the twelve years of knowing something was wrong and choosing not to name it.
"Prove it," she said.

"The jade pendant you're wearing." He nodded toward the artifact at her throat. "It's not a family heirloom. It's the third of seven nodes required to activate the dimensional gateway. When I stood near it during the memorial ceremony, it resonated with me‚Äîbecause the Spousal recognition the array grants me creates a partial bloodline signature. The pendant is a piece of the apocalypse, and you've been wearing it since your mother gave it to you."
Xue's hand flew to the pendant. Her knuckles were white.
"My mother gave it to me on her deathbed," she said hoarsely. "She said it would protect me."

"Maybe she believed that. Maybe she was told that. Or maybe‚Äî" Ren hesitated. "Maybe she was trying to keep one of the nodes out of your father's hands by giving it to the one person he wouldn't take it from."
The implication hit Xue like a physical blow. Her mother‚Äîdead under circumstances no one discussed‚Äîmight have been *protecting* her. Might have known. Might have died because she knew.

"I need to sit down." Xue's legs gave way before she could reach the bench. Ren caught her arm‚Äîsteadied her, guided her to the stone seat beside the koi pond. For a moment, her hand gripped his forearm, and he could feel the fine tremor running through her body.
"Your mother," Ren said gently, "may have been the first person to try to stop this."

Xue's eyes were wet. She didn't let the tears fall‚ÄîLiƒÅng women didn't cry in gardens, no matter what they'd just learned about their family. But the effort it took was visible, and something in Ren's chest ached for her.

"I wanted to believe I was the one protecting my family," she said. "Running the eastern division, blocking hostile takeovers, keeping my brother out of trouble. I thought if I was useful enough, valuable enough, the family would..." She trailed off.
"Would what?"

"Would be worth protecting." A bitter laugh. "Instead, I'm wearing a doomsday device around my neck and my brother is‚Äî" Her voice broke. "There's something wrong with Feng. I've known for months. I didn't want to see it."
"I know about Feng."
Her head snapped up. "What?"

"His dreams. His rages. The way his eyes flicker sometimes‚Äînot a trick of the light, not fatigue. Something is inside him, Xue. A parasite. An infection from the dimension the portal is designed to open. In the language of the people who understand these things, he's at Stage 2 of a spore infection. Stage 3 is irreversible."
The color drained from her face. "Can he be saved?"

"Yes. But it requires things I don't have yet‚Äîaccess to the family's cleansing arrays, and enough power to force the parasite into dormancy during a treatment cycle." Ren gripped her hand‚Äînot gently, but firmly. The way he'd once gripped the hands of soldiers who needed to hear the truth before a battle. "I'm going to save him. But I need time, and I need you to trust me."
"You're asking me to trust a man who won't tell me how he knows all of this."
"I'm asking you to trust the results. The shipping problem. The assassination. The mole. Every time I've acted, it's been to protect you and this family. That's not going to change."
Xue pulled her hand away. For a long moment, she stared at the koi pond, watching the fish circle in their endless, oblivious loops.
Then she turned back to him with a decision in her eyes.

"I won't sign the divorce papers. Not because I trust you‚Äîbecause I *need* to understand what you've shown me. And I can't do that from outside this family." She touched the pendant again‚Äîdeliberately this time, not reflexively. "If this is what you say it is, then keeping it away from my father is the most important thing I can do. And if you're lying to me..."
"I'm not."

"Then you have nothing to worry about." She stood, smoothing her dress with hands that had steadied. "But Ren‚Äîdon't think for a moment that I'm doing this because of our marriage. I'm doing this because someone has to, and apparently no one else in thisfamily is going to."
The Salvation System pulsed:

`CAUSAL BACKDOOR: REINFORCED`
`SUBJECT TRUST METRIC: 41% ‚Üí 56%`
`RELATIONSHIP STATUS: Strategic alliance ‚Üí Active conspiracy`
`KEY DEVELOPMENT: Liang Xue now KNOWS about the portal (partial disclosure). She is choosing to stay in the marriage based on information, not obligation.`
`COUNTDOWN UPDATE: 1085 days`
`NOTE: "Causal backdoor reinforced" ‚Äî the marriage bond is no longer just a legal technicality. It's becoming a genuine partnership, built on shared knowledge and common purpose. This makes it STRONGER as a causal firewall.`
Fifty-six percent. Above half. And the foundation had shifted from obligation to choice.
Xue walked back toward the estate. At the edge of the garden, she paused.

"The restricted wing." She spoke without turning around. "My mother died there. The official story is a heart condition, but she was thirty-nine and in perfect health. I've never been allowed inside." A pause. "When you're ready to go in... I want to be with you."
Then she was gone, swallowed by the estate's golden windows and ancient shadows.

Ren sat alone by the koi pond as the last light faded from the sky. The jade token was warm in his pocket. The jade pendant's resonance still hummed in his enhanced hearing. And somewhere beneath the manicured gardens and polished marble, the dimensional array counted down.
His phone buzzed. Liu Haoran.
*"The bronze bell arrives tomorrow. Node 5. Transport convoy, western route, 3 AM."*
Ren typed: *"Can we intercept?"*
*"Not without exposing yourself. The convoy has a spore-infected escort. Stage 3. You can't fight that at 1% power."*
*"Then what?"*
*"Watch. Learn. And find out where they store it. Because Node 6 is still out there, Zhou. And if we can get to it before the Patriarch does..."*
*"We buy time."*
*"We buy everything."*
Ren pocketed the phone. The stars were coming out over Crystal City‚Äîmillions of lights that had no idea what was building beneath them.

He had fifty-six percent of Liang Xue's trust. One percent of his power. Eighty-five days until the bronze bell was integrated into the array. Nine months until Feng's spore passed the point of no return.
And a restricted wing he was finally going to have help breaking into.
One step at a time. One day at a time. One impossible calculation after another.
The War God had returned. And the war was just beginning.
One thousand eighty-five days.
